text.title: Rate Floor — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for the rate-floor formula, sensitivity, and prospect-facing defence.

rule.statement: Billable % must come from historical data when ≥ 6 months exist; otherwise ceiling at 55% of working hours. A solo claiming 80% billable is statistically wrong and the formula MUST cap inputs at 55% if no data is provided.
rule.rationale: Self-reported billable hours run 45-55% of working hours across solo freelancers. The remaining 45-55% is sales, admin, learning, and unpaid rework. Optimistic inputs produce floors that bankrupt the operator.

rule.statement: Tax loading must include income tax + self-employment / social-security tax + state/local tax + sales/VAT (if not pass-through). US default load = 32-38%, EU default load = 40-50%, UK default load = 30-42%.

rationale.source: IRS Pub 334 / HMRC self-employment / EU directive 2017/2455

rule.rationale: A US solo who plans on "25% tax" forgets the 15.3% SE tax. Floor calculated on income tax alone underprices the year by ~$8k on $80k target.

rule.statement: Working weeks per year MUST be ≤ 46 of 52. Account for 4 weeks vacation/sick + 2 weeks holiday + 1 week buffer. Computing on 50+ weeks is optimistic; methodology rejects inputs > 46.

rationale.source: Healthy-solo cohort observation (Justin Jackson, Tara Reed essays)

rule.rationale: Solos who price for 52 working weeks burn out and lose Q4 revenue to crashes. Building the year around 46 weeks improves health AND average MRR.

rule.statement: Profit margin (above operator salary) must be ≥ 15%. The margin funds tooling, downtime, training, and the option to refuse bad work. A floor without profit is a wage, not a business.
rule.rationale: Solos without margin operate hand-to-mouth, cannot say no to bad clients, and never accumulate the runway to make strategic moves. 15% is the minimum that funds optionality.

rule.statement: Output MUST include 3 sensitivity bands: floor at billable_pct − 10pp, baseline, billable_pct + 10pp. Operator quotes the BASELINE; competitors only beat the floor if they're losing money.
rule.rationale: Operators want a single number. They get into trouble when the single number assumes one billable %. The 3-band output makes the math visible to operator AND prospect.

example.title: Loaded floor formula
example.code:
target_income = G (target gross) profit_margin = m (≥ 0.15) tax_load = t (e.g. 0.34 US) benefit_cost = B (annual, health/retirement) tooling_cost = T (annual subs) working_weeks = W (≤ 46) billable_pct = p (≤ 0.55 if no data)
needed_revenue_pre_tax = (G + B + T) × (1 + m) / (1 - t) billable_hours_per_year = W × 40 × p floor_usd_per_hour = needed_revenue_pre_tax / billable_hours_per_year

example.title: Naive formula (r1+r2+r3 violations)
example.code:
floor = target_income / (52 × 40) = $120k / 2080 = $58/hr
Misses tax, downtime, billable %, benefits, tooling, profit. Floor is ~2.5× too low. Operator goes broke at this rate.
example.note: This is the floor most solo "calculator" sites produce. It's effectively wage math, not business math.
